report_name: Ten Nations Warn of China-Linked Threat Actors Using Covert SOHO Botnets for Espionage

threat_actors.name: Volt Typhoon
threat_actors.description: A China-nexus APT group linked to the KV Botnet, targeting U.S. critical infrastructure by using compromised SOHO devices as a covert network.

threat_actors.name: Flax Typhoon
threat_actors.description: A China-nexus APT group linked to the Raptor Train botnet, conducting operations against Taiwan.

threat_actors.name: China-nexus threat actors
threat_actors.description: A collective term for multiple state-sponsored groups originating from China that leverage large-scale botnets of compromised edge devices for espionage and cyberattacks.

threat_actors.name: Integrity Technology Group
threat_actors.description: A Chinese information security company reported to be involved in building and maintaining covert botnet infrastructure for state use.

attack_patterns.name: External Proxy
attack_patterns.description: Compromised SOHO routers were used as a chain of external proxies to tunnel malicious traffic, making it appear to originate from residential or small business IP addresses.

attack_patterns.name: Exploit Public-Facing Application
attack_patterns.description: Threat actors exploited known or zero-day vulnerabilities in public-facing SOHO routers, firewalls, and IoT devices to gain initial access and incorporate them into botnets.

attack_patterns.name: Web Protocols
attack_patterns.description: Command and control and data exfiltration traffic was tunneled over standard web protocols like HTTP/HTTPS to blend in with normal network traffic.

attack_patterns.name: Acquire Infrastructure
attack_patterns.description: The threat actors acquired a vast network of vulnerable SOHO and IoT devices to build their operational infrastructure for launching attacks.

malware.name: KV Botnet
malware.description: A botnet comprised of vulnerable Cisco and NetGear routers, used by the Volt Typhoon group to attack U.S. critical infrastructure.

malware.name: Raptor Train
malware.description: A botnet network of over 200,000 devices linked to the Flax Typhoon group's operations against Taiwan.

tools.name: sshd
tools.description: Unauthorized remote access service that threat actors may run on compromised routers to maintain access.

tools.name: dropbear
tools.description: Unauthorized remote access service (lightweight SSH server) that threat actors may run on compromised routers.

tools.name: telnetd
tools.description: Unauthorized and insecure remote access service that threat actors may run on compromised routers.

identities.name: U.S. critical infrastructure
identities.description: A primary target of the Volt Typhoon group, which used the KV Botnet for pre-positioning and potential disruptive attacks.

identities.name: Cisco
identities.description: Vendor whose routers were identified as being vulnerable and compromised to form part of the KV Botnet.

identities.name: NetGear
identities.description: Vendor whose routers were identified as being vulnerable and compromised to form part of the KV Botnet.

infrastructure.name: Covert SOHO Network
infrastructure.description: A large-scale, dynamically managed network of compromised SOHO routers, firewalls, and IoT devices used by China-nexus actors to obfuscate their operations.

infrastructure.name: KV Botnet Infrastructure
infrastructure.description: The network infrastructure of the KV Botnet, used by Volt Typhoon as a proxy network for attacks.

infrastructure.name: Raptor Train Infrastructure
infrastructure.description: The network infrastructure of the Raptor Train botnet, used by Flax Typhoon for operations.

indicators.name: Anomalous Outbound Traffic from SOHO Edge Device
indicators.pattern:
title: Anomalous Outbound Traffic from SOHO Edge Device to Internal Network
id: 2b1a9c8d-e3f4-4a5b-8c6d-7e8f9a0b1c2d
status: experimental
description: |
  Detects network traffic originating from a SOHO edge device (router, firewall) to an internal network asset. This is highly anomalous as edge devices should typically not initiate connections inwards.
references:
  - https://www.nsa.gov/Press-Room/Press-Releases-Statements/Press-Release-View/Article/3752538/nsa-and-others-release-joint-guidance-addressing-multiple-china-nexus-threat-a/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-24
tags:
  - attack.t1090.002
level: high
logsource:
  category: firewall
detection:
  selection:
    src_ip_category: 'soho_device_range'
    dst_ip_category: 'internal_asset_range'
    action: 'allow'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate remote administration or monitoring tools initiating connections from the edge device.
  - Misconfigured network segments.
indicators.description: Detects network traffic patterns indicative of a compromised SOHO device being used as a proxy, specifically when it initiates connections to internal network assets.

indicators.name: Non-Standard Protocol Over Common Port
indicators.pattern:
title: Non-Standard Protocol Over Common Port
id: f8e7d6c5-b4a3-4987-6543-210987fedcba
status: experimental
description: |
  Detects traffic over common ports (e.g., 80, 443) that does not conform to the expected protocol (e.g., HTTP/TLS). This can indicate C2 traffic being tunneled to evade simple port-based firewall rules.
references:
  - https://www.nsa.gov/Press-Room/Press-Releases-Statements/Press-Release-View/Article/3752538/nsa-and-others-release-joint-guidance-addressing-multiple-china-nexus-threat-a/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-24
tags:
  - attack.t1071.001
level: medium
logsource:
  category: network_connection
  product: zeek
detection:
  selection:
    - destination_port: 443
      service|endswith: '!ssl'
    - destination_port: 80
      service|endswith: '!http'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Custom applications using non-standard port assignments.
indicators.description: Detects potential C2 tunneling by identifying traffic on common web ports (like 443) that is not valid for the expected protocol (e.g., not TLS).

indicators.name: Unauthorized Remote Access Services on Edge Devices
indicators.pattern:
title: Unauthorized Remote Access Services on Edge Devices
id: 1a2b3c4d-5e6f-7a8b-9c0d-1e2f3a4b5c6d
status: experimental
description: |
  Detects the execution of remote access daemons such as sshd, dropbear, or telnetd on network edge devices where they are not expected to be running. This could indicate a compromised router.
references:
  - https://www.nsa.gov/Press-Room/Press-Releases-Statements/Press-Release-View/Article/3752538/nsa-and-others-release-joint-guidance-addressing-multiple-china-nexus-threat-a/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-24
tags:
  - attack.t1021
level: high
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: linux
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith:
      - '/sshd'
      - '/dropbear'
      - '/telnetd'
  filter_known_good:
    ParentImage|endswith:
      - '/init'
      - '/systemd'
  condition: selection and not filter_known_good
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate administrative action or device startup processes.
indicators.description: Detects the execution of unexpected remote access services like sshd, dropbear, or telnetd on SOHO routers, which may indicate compromise.

relationships.source_name: Anomalous Outbound Traffic from SOHO Edge Device
Unauthorized Remote Access Services on Edge Devices
